<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Data Science Project** (in the KTU PBADT304 context) is a structured, end-to-end implementation exercise that requires the student to identify a real-world problem domain, acquire a raw dataset, store it in an appropriate data structure, perform systematic **data pre-processing operations** (data cleaning, missing value management, transformation, and integration), and apply small-scale **data mining techniques** to recognize meaningful patterns, associations, anomalies, or trends. The project is expected to demonstrate the complete **Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD) pipeline** at a miniature scale, with socially relevant domains carrying additional academic weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight (Verbatim Syllabus Intent):**
> *"Implement various data-related projects from any domain... It may contain sections for **data storage, data pre-processing and small levels of data mining to recognize patterns in the data**."*
> Socially relevant projects are **HIGHLY APPRECIATED** — meaning evaluators award bonus credit for domains like healthcare, agriculture, education, environment, and public welfare.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Detective's Investigation"

Think of a Data Science project like a **detective solving a mystery case**:

| Detective's World | Data Science World |
|---|---|
| Crime scene | Raw dataset |
| Collecting fingerprints & evidence | **Data acquisition** (e.g., Kaggle download) |
| Locker room (evidence storage) | **Data storage** (CSV, SQL DB, DataFrame) |
| Removing dust, false prints | **Data cleaning** (noise, duplicates removal) |
| Filling in torn paper clues | **Missing value management** (imputation) |
| Organizing evidence on a board | **Data transformation** (normalization, encoding) |
| Finding "the butler did it" pattern | **Data mining / pattern recognition** |
| Writing the case report | **Insight presentation & visualization** |

Just as a detective cannot jump straight to the conclusion without preparing evidence, you cannot feed raw, dirty data into a model. **80% of a data scientist's time is spent on data preparation** — and this is exactly what Module 4 trains you to do.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 80/20 Rule of Data Science (Domain Standard Metric):** Industry surveys (IBM, Kaggle) consistently report that **80%** of a data scientist's working time is consumed by data acquisition, cleaning, and pre-processing. Only the remaining **20%** goes into actual modeling and mining. Your KTU project reflects this reality.

## 1.3 Project Anatomy: The Three Mandatory Slices

Every KTU-eligible project MUST structurally contain these three sections, as explicitly stated in the syllabus:

1. **Data Storage Section** — Where and how the data is persisted (file format, DBMS, cloud, DataFrame).
2. **Data Pre-processing Section** — Data cleaning, missing value imputation, transformation, integration, reduction.
3. **Data Mining Section** — Small-level application of pattern recognition techniques (classification, clustering, association, anomaly detection, or trend analysis).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** A project that *skips* any of the three slices above typically loses **2–3 marks** on the first read itself. Always have explicit headings: `3.1 Data Storage`, `3.2 Data Pre-processing`, `3.3 Data Mining`.

---

## 1.4 What is a "Socially Relevant" Project?

A project is **socially relevant** if its findings or tool can benefit the **public good**, marginalized communities, or sustainable development goals (aligned with the United Nations **17 SDGs**). Examples:

- Predicting crop yield for small-scale farmers (SDG 2: Zero Hunger)
- Detecting fake news to protect democratic discourse (SDG 16: Peace, Justice)
- Forecasting water-borne disease outbreaks (SDG 6: Clean Water)
- Predicting student dropout rates in rural schools (SDG 4: Quality Education)
- Identifying illegal waste-dumping sites via satellite imagery (SDG 11: Sustainable Cities)
- Sentiment analysis on mental-health support forums (SDG 3: Good Health)
- Forecasting air pollution levels (SDG 13: Climate Action)
- Loan default prediction for microfinance (SDG 1: No Poverty)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Socially relevant does NOT mean social media analytics on trending topics. It means the project has a tangible positive impact on a community, environment, or vulnerable population.

---

## 1.5 Datasets: Where Do Projects Get Their Data?

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mentions **Kaggle** (`https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/`). However, the following are equally accepted and industry-standard:

| Source | URL Pattern | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Kaggle Datasets** | `kaggle.com/datasets` | Clean CSV, beginner-friendly |
| **UCI Machine Learning Repository** | `archive.ics.uci.edu` | Academic, classic ML datasets |
| **data.gov (India Open Data)** | `data.gov.in` | Government, civic, socio-economic |
| **Google Dataset Search** | `datasetsearch.research.google.com` | Meta-search across sources |
| **World Bank Open Data** | `data.worldbank.org` | Development indicators |
| **WHO Global Health Observatory** | `apps.who.int/gho/data` | Health-related projects |
| **Kerala State Open Data** | `kerala.data.gov.in` | Kerala-specific socio projects |
| **GitHub Public Datasets** | `github.com` | Raw, real-world messy data |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Downloading a dataset from Kaggle and **never** doing any pre-processing. Examiners explicitly look for evidence of cleaning, missing-value handling, and transformation. If your dataset is "already clean," you must still demonstrate the *pre-processing methodology* you *would* apply (e.g., explain the pipeline on a sample).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CRISP-DM Framework: The Backbone of Every Data Science Project

**CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** is the de-facto industrial methodology for executing data science projects. It has **6 phases**, all of which a KTU project must traverse (at a miniature scale):

1. **Business Understanding** — Define the problem.
2. **Data Understanding** — Collect & explore the data.
3. **Data Preparation** — Clean, transform, integrate.
4. **Modeling** — Apply data mining algorithms.
5. **Evaluation** — Validate results against business goal.
6. **Deployment** — Present insights / build an app.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Module 4 project scope maps directly to **phases 1, 2, 3, and a sliver of 4–5** (small-level data mining + insight generation). Full deployment is not expected.

---

## 2.2 Data Storage: Choosing the Right Vessel

The "data storage" slice of your project can take any of these forms:

| Storage Type | Format / Tool | When to Use | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat File (CSV)** | `.csv`, `.tsv` | Small tabular data | Simple, human-readable | Slow for big data |
| **Excel Workbook** | `.xlsx` | Mixed data types, small | GUI-friendly | Row limit 1,048,576 |
| **SQL Database** | SQLite, MySQL | Structured, relational | Queryable, scalable | Setup overhead |
| **NoSQL Document Store** | MongoDB | Semi-structured JSON | Schema-flexible | Less analytics-friendly |
| **In-Memory** | Pandas DataFrame | Mid-size analysis | Fast operations | Volatile |
| **Cloud Storage** | AWS S3, Google Drive | Large / public datasets | Scalable | Requires internet |
| **Data Lake** | Parquet, ORC files | Big data projects | Columnar, compressed | Specialized tools |

> [!TIP]
> For a typical KTU B.Tech project (CS branch, 2024 scheme), the **Pandas DataFrame in a Jupyter Notebook** with a backup **CSV file** is the most pragmatic and examiner-friendly choice.

---

## 2.3 Data Pre-Processing: The Core Knowledge Area

Data pre-processing is the **single most important knowledge area** evaluated in Module 4. The KTU syllabus specifies two sub-tasks explicitly: **data cleaning** and **missing value management**. A complete project also touches on transformation, integration, and reduction.

### 2.3.1 Data Cleaning Operations

- **Handling noisy data** — Binning, regression, clustering-based smoothing.
- **Removing duplicates** — Identifying identical rows.
- **Fixing inconsistencies** — "Male" vs "M" vs "male" in a Gender column.
- **Outlier detection** — Z-score, IQR, box-plot based.
- **Typo / format correction** — Standardizing date formats, units, casing.

### 2.3.2 Missing Value Management

The four classical strategies, with **formulas** for each imputation method:

### 2.3.3 Data Transformation

- **Min-Max Normalization** (scales to [0, 1]):

$$
x_{norm} = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}
$$

- **Z-Score Standardization** (zero mean, unit variance):

$$
z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

- **Decimal Scaling**:

$$
x_{scaled} = \frac{x}{10^{j}}
$$

> where $j$ is the smallest integer such that $\max \vert x_{scaled} \vert \le 1$.

- **One-Hot Encoding** — for categorical features.
- **Discretization** — binning continuous variables into intervals.

---

## 2.4 Data Mining: Pattern Recognition Techniques (Small Level)

The "small levels of data mining" requirement from the KTU syllabus implies **at least one** of the following light-weight techniques:

| Technique | Output | Algorithm Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | Predict a discrete label | Decision Tree, k-NN, Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression |
| **Clustering** | Group similar records | k-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN |
| **Association Rule Mining** | Discover co-occurrence rules | Apriori, FP-Growth |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Spot rare / unusual data | Isolation Forest, Z-score outliers |
| **Regression** | Predict a continuous value | Linear Regression, Polynomial Regression |
| **Trend / Time-Series Analysis** | Find temporal patterns | Moving average, ARIMA (basic) |
| **Text Mining / NLP** | Extract meaning from text | Tokenization, TF-IDF, Sentiment scoring |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Formula | Use Case | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean imputation | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Numerical, symmetric distribution | Sensitive to outliers |
| 2 | Median imputation | $\tilde{x} = \text{median}(x)$ | Numerical, skewed distribution | Robust to outliers |
| 3 | Mode imputation | $\hat{x}_{mode} = \arg\max_{x} f(x)$ | Categorical features | Works for non-numeric |
| 4 | Forward fill | $x_t = x_{t-1}$ | Time-series | Preserves trend |
| 5 | Min-Max Normalization | $x' = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | Scale to [0, 1] | Bounded, sensitive to outliers |
| 6 | Z-Score Standardization | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Zero mean, unit variance | Unbounded output |
| 7 | Decimal Scaling | $x' = \frac{x}{10^{j}}$ | Quick magnitude compression | $j$ chosen manually |
| 8 | IQR Outlier Rule | $x < Q1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR$ or $x > Q3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR$ | Outlier detection | $IQR = Q3 - Q1$ |
| 9 | Z-Score Outlier Rule | $\vert z \vert > 3$ | Outlier detection (Gaussian data) | Assumes normal distribution |
| 10 | Entropy (Information) | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Decision Tree splits | Bits |
| 11 | Information Gain | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in V(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Feature selection | Bits |
| 12 | Gini Impurity | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | CART trees | Range [0, 0.5] |
| 13 | Euclidean Distance | $d(p,q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(p_i - q_i)^2}$ | k-NN, k-Means | Same units as features |
| 14 | Manhattan Distance | $d(p,q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_i - q_i \vert \vert$ | k-NN, sparse data | Sum of absolute diffs |
| 15 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{p \cdot q}{\Vert p \Vert \cdot \Vert q \Vert}$ | Text mining | Range [-1, 1] |
| 16 | Support (Association) | $supp(X) = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{\text{total transactions}}$ | Apriori algorithm | Range [0, 1] |
| 17 | Confidence (Association) | $conf(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{supp(X \cup Y)}{supp(X)}$ | Rule strength | Range [0, 1] |
| 18 | Lift (Association) | $lift(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{conf(X \Rightarrow Y)}{supp(Y)}$ | Rule interestingness | >1 = positive correlation |
| 19 | Precision | $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Classification quality | Higher is better |
| 20 | Recall | $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Classification coverage | Higher is better |
| 21 | F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Balance P and R | Harmonic mean |
| 22 | Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression quality | Squared units of $y$ |
| 23 | R-Squared | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Variance explained | Range [0, 1] |
| 24 | TF-IDF | $tf\text{-}idf(t,d) = tf(t,d) \cdot \log\frac{N}{df(t)}$ | Text mining weight | Higher = more unique |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 14-mark questions**, you will be expected to apply 2–3 of these formulas in context. Memorize the **bolded** ones (1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 8, 10, 11, 13, 19, 20, 21) at minimum.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Project Implementation Framework & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The 10-Step KTU Project Execution Blueprint

Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step procedure** to execute a KTU 2024 data science project. Every step is mandatory. Skipping a step is a guaranteed deduction.

---

### **Step 1 — Problem Definition & Domain Selection**

Choose ONE domain. Socially relevant domains are preferred. Frame the problem as a single research question.

*Example:* "Can we predict whether a student in a Kerala government school will drop out, based on attendance, marks, and family income data?"

Document: (a) The problem statement, (b) Why it is socially relevant, (c) The expected impact.

---

### **Step 2 — Dataset Acquisition**

Visit `https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/`, search keywords related to your domain. Download the CSV/JSON. Save to a `/data` folder.

*Example search:* "kerala education", "india student dropout", "crop yield india".

Record the source URL, license, and number of rows/columns in your report.

---

### **Step 3 — Data Storage Setup**

Decide on a storage mechanism. For most KTU projects, **Pandas DataFrame in a Jupyter Notebook** with a backup **CSV file** is sufficient. If the dataset is relational, use **SQLite**. If the data is JSON, use **MongoDB** or simply `pd.read_json()`.

Record the storage decision with a brief justification.

---

### **Step 4 — Data Understanding (Exploratory Data Analysis — EDA)**

This is your "first look" at the data. Document:

- `df.shape` — number of rows and columns
- `df.dtypes` — column types
- `df.describe()` — mean, std, min, max, quartiles for numerical
- `df.describe(include='O')` — top, freq, unique for categorical
- `df.isnull().sum()` — count of missing values per column
- `df.nunique()` — count of unique values per column
- Sample rows: `df.head()` and `df.tail()`

> [!TIP]
> Always include at least **3 visualizations** in this step: a histogram, a box-plot, and a correlation heatmap. Visual evidence of EDA is what convinces the examiner.

---

### **Step 5 — Data Cleaning**

Apply the cleaning pipeline you designed. Document each operation:

**5.1 Remove duplicates:**

```text
df = df.drop_duplicates()
```

**5.2 Standardize inconsistent values:**

```text
df['Gender'] = df['Gender'].str.upper().str.strip()
df['Gender'] = df['Gender'].replace({'M': 'MALE', 'F': 'FEMALE'})
```

**5.3 Correct data types:**

```text
df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['Date'], errors='coerce')
df['Marks'] = pd.to_numeric(df['Marks'], errors='coerce')
```

**5.4 Detect & handle outliers (IQR method):**

$$
Q1 = \text{25th percentile}, \quad Q3 = \text{75th percentile}
$$

$$
IQR = Q3 - Q1
$$

$$
\text{Lower fence} = Q1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR
$$

$$
\text{Upper fence} = Q3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR
$$

Any $x < \text{Lower fence}$ or $x > \text{Upper fence}$ is an outlier. Decide: drop, cap (winsorize), or keep.

**5.5 Filter irrelevant rows/columns:**

```text
df = df.drop(columns=['Irrelevant_Col'])
df = df[df['Year'] >= 2010]
```

---

### **Step 6 — Missing Value Management (THE MOST VALUABLE STEP)**

This step has the highest weight in the KTU project evaluation. Show the missing-value matrix first:

```text
import missingno as msno
msno.matrix(df)
msno.heatmap(df)
```

Then apply the appropriate imputation for each column. **The choice of method depends on data type and distribution:**

| Column Type | Recommended Imputation | Formula Used |
|---|---|---|
| Numerical, symmetric, no outliers | Mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ |
| Numerical, skewed or with outliers | Median | $\tilde{x} = \text{median}(x)$ |
| Categorical | Mode | Most frequent value |
| Time-series | Forward fill / Interpolation | $x_t = x_{t-1}$ |
| High-missingness (>40%) | Drop column | — |

**Worked Numerical Example for Mean Imputation:**

Suppose the "Marks" column has 5 missing values and the remaining 95 non-null values sum to 4750.

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{4750}{95} = 50.0
$$

Replace all 5 missing values with **50.0**.

**Worked Numerical Example for Median Imputation:**

The sorted "Income" values (in ₹ thousands) for a column with missing entries:

$$
\{10, 12, 13, 15, 15, 15, 16, 18, 20, 22, 25, 100\}
$$

The middle value (since $n=12$, average of 6th and 7th terms) is:

$$
\tilde{x} = \frac{15 + 16}{2} = 15.5
$$

The ₹100 value (outlier) is bypassed. Replace missing values with **15.5**.

---

### **Step 7 — Data Transformation**

Apply normalization or encoding as needed:

**7.1 Min-Max Normalization (worked example):**

Given $x = 80$, $x_{min} = 20$, $x_{max} = 120$:

$$
x_{norm} = \frac{80 - 20}{120 - 20} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.6
$$

**7.2 Z-Score Standardization (worked example):**

Given $x = 80$, $\mu = 60$, $\sigma = 10$:

$$
z = \frac{80 - 60}{10} = 2.0
$$

This value lies **2 standard deviations above the mean**.

**7.3 One-Hot Encoding (worked example):**

For a "Department" column with values $\{CS, EC, ME\}$, one-hot encoding produces:

| Department_CS | Department_EC | Department_ME |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 0 | 0 | 1 |

---

### **Step 8 — Data Mining (Pattern Recognition)**

Pick ONE small-level data mining task:

**Option A — Classification (Decision Tree):**
- Use features: attendance, family_income, marks
- Use label: dropout (Yes/No)
- Apply a Decision Tree classifier
- Report accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score

**Option B — Clustering (k-Means):**
- Use features: rainfall, fertilizer, soil_pH
- Group districts into 3 clusters: high-yield, medium, low
- Use the elbow method to choose $k$

**Option C — Association Rule Mining (Apriori):**
- Use transactions: items bought together in a market
- Find rules: $\{$Bread, Butter$\} \Rightarrow \{$Milk$\}$
- Compute support, confidence, lift

**Option D — Anomaly Detection:**
- Use credit card transaction data
- Apply Isolation Forest
- Flag transactions with anomaly score $< -0.1$

For all options, report the **metric formulas** and a **pattern summary**.

---

### **Step 9 — Insight Generation & Visualization**

Produce at least 4 plots:

1. **Bar chart** — class distribution
2. **Heatmap** — correlation matrix
3. **Box plot** — pre vs post-outlier removal
4. **Scatter plot** — relationship between two key features
5. **Confusion matrix** (if classification) — colored heatmap
6. **Word cloud** (if text mining) — frequent terms

State **3–5 actionable insights** derived from the data. Example:

> *"Districts with rainfall < 50 cm AND soil pH > 8 show 67% lower crop yield. Recommend soil treatment for these regions."*

---

### **Step 10 — Documentation & Presentation**

Structure your final report:

```text
1. Title Page
2. Abstract (150 words)
3. Table of Contents
4. Chapter 1 — Introduction & Problem Definition
5. Chapter 2 — Dataset Description & Data Storage
6. Chapter 3 — Data Pre-processing
   3.1 Data Cleaning
   3.2 Missing Value Management
   3.3 Data Transformation
7. Chapter 4 — Data Mining & Pattern Recognition
8. Chapter 5 — Results & Visualizations
9. Chapter 6 — Conclusion & Future Scope
10. References (Kaggle URL, library docs)
```

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Why Mean Imputation Reduces Variance

We want to show formally why mean imputation is **biased but variance-reducing**.

Let $X$ be a column with $n$ total observations, of which $k$ are missing. Let $\bar{x}$ be computed on the $n - k$ observed values. After mean imputation:

**Bias introduced:**

$$
E[\bar{x}_{imp}] - E[X] = E[\bar{x}] - E[X] = 0
$$

So the **mean is unbiased** (when data is MCAR — Missing Completely At Random).

**Variance after imputation:**

$$
Var(\bar{x}_{imp}) = \frac{(n-k) \cdot s^2 + k \cdot 0}{n \cdot n} = \frac{(n-k) s^2}{n^2}
$$

The variance of the **filled column** (as a set) is:

$$
Var_{filled} = \frac{(n-k) \cdot s^2}{n}
$$

Compared to the **true variance** of the original column:

$$
Var_{true} = \frac{n \cdot \sigma^2}{n} = \sigma^2
$$

We can prove that the filled-in column **underestimates variance** because the imputed values (all equal to $\bar{x}$) contribute zero variance:

$$
Var_{filled} = \frac{(n-k) \cdot s^2}{n} < \frac{n \cdot s^2}{n} = s^2
$$

**Conclusion:** Mean imputation is a **trade-off** — it preserves the mean but artificially deflates variance and correlations. This is why advanced techniques like **MICE (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations)** or **KNN imputation** are preferred in research, but for KTU project-level work, mean/median/mode is acceptable.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Information Gain (Used in Decision Trees)

For a dataset $S$ with $c$ classes and class probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_c$:

**Entropy (impurity) of $S$:**

$$
H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

**Information Gain of splitting $S$ on attribute $A$** which partitions $S$ into subsets $\{S_v \mid v \in V(A)\}$:

$$
IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in V(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot H(S_v)
$$

**Worked example:** Let $S$ = 14 records: 9 "Yes", 5 "No".

$$
H(S) = -\left(\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} + \frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14}\right)
$$

Computing each term:

$$
-\frac{9}{14}\log_2\frac{9}{14} \approx -0.643 \cdot (-0.637) \approx 0.410
$$

$$
-\frac{5}{14}\log_2\frac{5}{14} \approx -0.357 \cdot (-1.485) \approx 0.530
$$

$$
H(S) \approx 0.410 + 0.530 = 0.940 \text{ bits}
$$

Now suppose attribute $A$ splits $S$ into $S_1$ (6 records: 4 Yes, 2 No) and $S_2$ (8 records: 5 Yes, 3 No):

$$
H(S_1) = -\left(\frac{4}{6}\log_2\frac{4}{6} + \frac{2}{6}\log_2\frac{2}{6}\right) \approx 0.918 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
H(S_2) = -\left(\frac{5}{8}\log_2\frac{5}{8} + \frac{3}{8}\log_2\frac{3}{8}\right) \approx 0.954 \text{ bits}
$$

Weighted entropy after split:

$$
H(S \mid A) = \frac{6}{14} \cdot 0.918 + \frac{8}{14} \cdot 0.954 \approx 0.393 + 0.545 = 0.938
$$

Information Gain:

$$
IG(S, A) = 0.940 - 0.938 = 0.002 \text{ bits}
$$

**Conclusion:** This attribute $A$ is a **very weak splitter** (almost no information gained). The decision tree algorithm will prefer an attribute with higher IG.

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation: Min-Max Normalization Inverse

Given the normalized value $x_{norm} \in [0, 1]$, to recover the original:

$$
x = x_{norm} \cdot (x_{max} - x_{min}) + x_{min}
$$

**Verification with our earlier example:** $x_{norm} = 0.6$, $x_{min} = 20$, $x_{max} = 120$:

$$
x = 0.6 \cdot (120 - 20) + 20 = 0.6 \cdot 100 + 20 = 60 + 20 = 80 \;\;\checkmark
$$

The inverse transformation is essential when **interpreting model outputs** in original units (e.g., predicted crop yield in tonnes/hectare).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Define "Data Pre-processing" and list any four operations performed during it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Data pre-processing is the phase in the data mining pipeline that transforms raw, real-world data into a clean, consistent, and analysis-ready format. It is critical because real datasets contain noise, missing values, inconsistencies, and outliers that degrade the quality of any subsequent mining operation.

Four operations:

1. **Data Cleaning** — removing noise, duplicates, and correcting inconsistencies.
2. **Missing Value Management** — imputing or dropping null entries using mean, median, mode, or interpolation.
3. **Data Transformation** — normalizing (Min-Max, Z-score), encoding (one-hot), or aggregating.
4. **Data Reduction** — dimensionality reduction (PCA), sampling, or feature selection.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for 4 operations = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**What is a "socially relevant" data science project? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A socially relevant data science project is one whose problem domain, methodology, or output directly benefits the **public good, marginalized communities, sustainable development, or environmental well-being** — typically aligned with the United Nations' 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

Two examples:

1. **Crop Yield Prediction for Small Farmers** — uses weather, soil, and fertilizer data to forecast yield, helping smallholders in India make better sowing decisions (SDG 2: Zero Hunger).
2. **Water Potability Classification** — predicts whether a given water source is safe to drink, helping rural communities prevent water-borne diseases (SDG 6: Clean Water and Sanitation).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for 2 examples = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **CRISP-DM methodology** with a neat diagram. List all six phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the various **data pre-processing techniques** in detail. For each technique, write the formula and a one-line use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** is the industry-standard, six-phase methodology for executing data science projects. It is iterative and cyclic — learnings from later phases can send the project back to an earlier phase.

**The six phases:**

1. **Business / Problem Understanding** — Define the project objectives from a business / social perspective, convert them into a data mining problem, and prepare an initial plan.
2. **Data Understanding** — Collect initial data, describe it, explore it (EDA), and verify its quality. Identify subsets, patterns, or anomalies.
3. **Data Preparation** — The most time-consuming phase (≈ 80% of effort). Tasks include data cleaning, missing-value handling, integration, transformation, and reduction to produce the final analytical dataset.
4. **Modeling** — Select and apply various data mining techniques (classification, clustering, association, regression). Calibrate parameters to optimal values.
5. **Evaluation** — Review the model thoroughly, assess whether it meets the original business objectives, and identify any unaddressed issues.
6. **Deployment** — Present the model as a report, deploy it as a live system, or hand it over to the customer. Plan for monitoring and maintenance.

**Diagram (must draw in exam):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Business Understanding] --> B[Data Understanding]
    B --> C[Data Preparation]
    C --> D[Modeling]
    D --> E[Evaluation]
    E --> F[Deployment]
    F -.-> A
```

The arrow from F back to A indicates that the process is **cyclic** and lessons learned in deployment can spawn new business questions.

*Rubric (7 marks):* 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per phase × 6 phases = 6 marks, plus 0 marks reserved for diagram mention (1 mark extra for clear diagram label).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Data Pre-Processing Techniques:**

**1. Data Cleaning:**
Removes noise, inconsistencies, duplicates, and outliers.

- **Mean Imputation** for missing numerical values:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

Use case: Filling missing "Age" values in a symmetric distribution.

- **Median Imputation** for skewed numerical data:

$$
\tilde{x} = \text{median}(x)
$$

Use case: Filling missing "Income" values when outliers are present.

- **Mode Imputation** for categorical data: most frequent value.

- **IQR Outlier Detection:**

$$
\text{Bound} = [Q1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR, \quad Q3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]
$$

Use case: Identifying and capping extreme exam scores.

**2. Data Integration:**
Combines data from multiple sources (databases, files, APIs) into a coherent dataset. Solves entity identification, redundancy, and value conflict problems.

**3. Data Transformation:**

- **Min-Max Normalization:**

$$
x' = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}
$$

Use case: Scaling pixel values to [0, 1] for image data.

- **Z-Score Standardization:**

$$
z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

Use case: Centering features with zero mean and unit variance before PCA.

- **One-Hot Encoding:** converts categorical variables into binary vectors. Use case: Converting "City" into multiple binary columns.

- **Discretization:** converts continuous to categorical (e.g., Age → Child/Adult/Senior).

**4. Data Reduction:**

- **Dimensionality Reduction (PCA)** — reduces feature space while preserving variance.
- **Numerosity Reduction** — replaces data with smaller representations (regression, histograms, clustering).
- **Sampling** — uses a representative subset of the full data.

*Rubric (7 marks):* 1 mark each for 4 technique categories × 1.5 marks, plus 1 mark for formulas. Total = 7 marks.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the **various missing value management techniques** with suitable formulas and examples. Mention when each technique should be preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail dataset has transactions from a local kirana store. Apply **Association Rule Mining** concepts. Compute the **support, confidence, and lift** for the rule $\{$Bread, Butter$\} \Rightarrow \{$Milk$\}$ given the following transactional summary (assume 1000 total transactions): **(7 marks)**

| Itemset | Count |
|---|---|
| Bread | 400 |
| Butter | 350 |
| Milk | 500 |
| $\{$Bread, Butter$\}$ | 150 |
| $\{$Bread, Milk$\}$ | 180 |
| $\{$Butter, Milk$\}$ | 170 |
| $\{$Bread, Butter, Milk$\}$ | 80 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Missing Value Management Techniques:**

**1. Deletion (Listwise / Pairwise):**
- **Listwise:** Drop any record with $\ge 1$ missing value. Pro: simple. Con: data loss.
- **Pairwise:** Use only complete cases for each analysis. Pro: retains more data. Con: results hard to compare.
- **Column drop:** Drop entire column if missingness > 40%.

**2. Mean Imputation:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n - k}\sum_{i \in \text{observed}} x_i
$$

Replace all missing entries with $\bar{x}$.

- *Preferred when:* Numerical, symmetric distribution, low missingness (< 10%), and data is MCAR.
- *Example:* "Age" of customers when the missing rate is 4%.

**3. Median Imputation:**

$$
\tilde{x} = \text{median of observed values}
$$

- *Preferred when:* Numerical, **skewed** distribution or has outliers.
- *Example:* "Income" of loan applicants.

**4. Mode Imputation:**

Replace missing values with the most frequent category.

- *Preferred when:* **Categorical** features.
- *Example:* Missing "Gender" filled with the most common value.

**5. Forward Fill / Backward Fill:**

$$
x_t = x_{t-1} \quad \text{(forward)}, \qquad x_t = x_{t+1} \quad \text{(backward)}
$$

- *Preferred when:* **Time-series** data.

**6. Interpolation (Linear / Polynomial):**

$$
x_t = x_{t-1} + \frac{(x_{t+1} - x_{t-1})}{(t+1) - (t-1)} \cdot (t - (t-1))
$$

- *Preferred when:* Continuous numerical time-series.

**7. KNN Imputation:**
Replace missing value with the mean of the $k$ nearest neighbors (by Euclidean distance). Use when advanced imputation is acceptable and $k$ is small (e.g., $k = 5$).

*Rubric (7 marks):* 1 mark each for 7 techniques, minus 1 mark if no formula. The 7th technique is bonus.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Support of individual items:**

$$
supp(\text{Bread}) = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.40
$$

$$
supp(\text{Butter}) = \frac{350}{1000} = 0.35
$$

$$
supp(\text{Milk}) = \frac{500}{1000} = 0.50
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Support of pairs:**

$$
supp(\{\text{Bread, Butter}\}) = \frac{150}{1000} = 0.15
$$

$$
supp(\{\text{Bread, Milk}\}) = \frac{180}{1000} = 0.18
$$

$$
supp(\{\text{Butter, Milk}\}) = \frac{170}{1000} = 0.17
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Support of the rule's full antecedent ∪ consequent:**

$$
supp(\{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\}) = \frac{80}{1000} = 0.08
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Confidence of the rule $\{$Bread, Butter$\} \Rightarrow \{$Milk$\}$:**

$$
conf(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{supp(X \cup Y)}{supp(X)} = \frac{supp(\{\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}\})}{supp(\{\text{Bread, Butter}\})}
$$

$$
conf(\{\text{Bread, Butter}\} \Rightarrow \{\text{Milk}\}) = \frac{0.08}{0.15} = 0.5333
$$

**Interpretation:** 53.33% of customers who buy both Bread and Butter also buy Milk.

**Step 5 — Compute Lift of the rule:**

$$
lift(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{conf(X \Rightarrow Y)}{supp(Y)} = \frac{0.5333}{0.50} = 1.0667
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $lift > 1$, the rule $\{$Bread, Butter$\} \Rightarrow \{$Milk$\}$ indicates a **positive correlation** — buying Bread and Butter increases the likelihood of buying Milk by a factor of 1.0667. Although the lift is only slightly above 1, the rule is still actionable for cross-merchandising in the kirana store.

*Rubric (7 marks):* 2 marks for support computations, 2 marks for confidence, 2 marks for lift, 1 mark for interpretation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the rule direction:** Students often compute $conf(\{$Bread, Milk$\} \Rightarrow \{$Butter$\})$ instead of the asked rule. Read the question's arrow carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by 1000:** Many students leave counts as-is. Support is a *fraction*, not a count. Always divide by total transactions.
> 3. **Not interpreting the lift value:** $lift > 1$ means positive correlation; $lift = 1$ means independent; $lift < 1$ means negative correlation. Without this interpretation, **1 mark is lost**.
> 4. **Writing the support of the antecedent only:** Some students confuse $supp(X)$ with $supp(X \cup Y)$. The rule's numerator is the **full itemset**, not just the antecedent.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in CRISP-DM:** In 7-mark theory questions, the diagram carries **at least 1 mark**. Always draw it.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4):**

- [ ] A KTU data science project **MUST** have three sections: **Data Storage, Data Pre-processing, Data Mining**.
- [ ] **CRISP-DM** has 6 phases: Business Understanding → Data Understanding → Data Preparation → Modeling → Evaluation → Deployment (cyclic).
- [ ] **80% of project effort** is data pre-processing — this is where the marks are.
- [ ] **Socially relevant** projects (Healthcare, Agriculture, Education, Environment, Finance for poor) carry **bonus credit**.
- [ ] **Kaggle** is the primary data source mentioned in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Backup options: UCI ML Repo, data.gov.in, WHO GHO.
- [ ] **Mean imputation** formula: $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ — use for symmetric, no-outlier data.
- [ ] **Median imputation** — use for skewed data with outliers.
- [ ] **Mode imputation** — use for categorical features.
- [ ] **Min-Max Normalization** formula: $x' = \frac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ — bounds to [0, 1].
- [ ] **Z-Score Standardization** formula: $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ — zero mean, unit variance.
- [ ] **IQR Outlier rule**: outside $[Q1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR, Q3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$.
- [ ] **Z-Score Outlier rule**: $\vert z \vert > 3$.
- [ ] **Entropy** formula: $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ — measures impurity.
- [ ] **Information Gain**: $IG = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ — used in Decision Trees.
- [ ] **Euclidean distance**: $d = \sqrt{\sum (p_i - q_i)^2}$.
- [ ] **Support** in association: $supp(X) = \frac{count(X)}{total}$.
- [ ] **Confidence** in association: $conf(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{supp(X \cup Y)}{supp(X)}$.
- [ ] **Lift** in association: $lift(X \Rightarrow Y) = \frac{conf(X \Rightarrow Y)}{supp(Y)}$. Lift > 1 → positive correlation.
- [ ] **Precision**: $P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$. **Recall**: $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$. **F1**: harmonic mean of P and R.
- [ ] **R-Squared** for regression: $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$.
- [ ] **TF-IDF** weight: $tf\text{-}idf = tf \cdot \log\frac{N}{df}$.
- [ ] Always **draw a diagram** for CRISP-DM / KDD / pipeline questions — minimum 1 mark guaranteed.
- [ ] Always **interpret** lift, accuracy, IG, etc. — not just compute. Interpretation carries 1 mark in every 14-mark question.
- [ ] **Socially relevant does NOT mean social media analytics.** It means real public-good impact (SDG-aligned).
- [ ] **Storage layer:** For most projects, Pandas DataFrame in Jupyter + CSV backup. Use SQLite for relational, MongoDB for JSON.
- [ ] **Data Mining "small level"** = at least one technique applied, with metric reported. Single algorithm is sufficient.
